evals.prompt: I'm writing a software raycaster in Odin using raylib. It's correct but slow — at 800x600 I get about 30 FPS even with simple scenes, and most of that time is spent in my per-pixel rendering. I'm using rl.DrawPixel inside the inner loop. What's the idiomatic raylib pattern for fast per-pixel rendering, and how would I rewrite the rendering setup? Show the full main-loop structure including buffer creation, per-pixel writes, the per-frame upload, and cleanup.
evals.expected_output: Identifies that rl.DrawPixel issues a GPU draw call per pixel and is the bottleneck. Recommends the rl.Image software-framebuffer pattern: build the frame in CPU memory with rl.ImageDrawPixel, upload once per frame with rl.UpdateTexture, blit with rl.DrawTexture, clear with rl.ImageClearBackground. Shows full main-loop structure with rl.GenImageColor for the framebuffer, rl.LoadTextureFromImage for the GPU texture, the per-frame UpdateTexture+DrawTexture+ImageClearBackground sequence, and matching defer rl.UnloadImage / rl.UnloadTexture for cleanup. Roughly 2x FPS improvement is realistic.
expectations: Identifies rl.DrawPixel as the bottleneck because each call goes through the GPU draw pipeline
Recommends the rl.Image (CPU framebuffer) pattern: write to image, upload once, blit
Uses rl.GenImageColor (or similar rl.Image-creating function) for the backing framebuffer
Uses rl.LoadTextureFromImage to create the GPU texture that mirrors the framebuffer
Uses rl.ImageDrawPixel (or equivalent direct image-data writes) for per-pixel writes, NOT rl.DrawPixel
Uses rl.UpdateTexture once per frame to upload the framebuffer to the GPU texture
Uses rl.DrawTexture to blit the texture to the screen each frame
Mentions clearing the framebuffer each frame (rl.ImageClearBackground or equivalent)
Shows a complete main-loop structure from window init through cleanup
Pairs allocations with defer for cleanup (rl.UnloadImage, rl.UnloadTexture, rl.CloseWindow)

evals.prompt: Show me how to load a PNG texture in Odin using raylib's bindings and read individual pixel colors from it for custom CPU-side rendering (texture sampling, blitting, processing — I don't want raylib to draw it for me, I just want the pixel array). Cover: loading the file, getting at the pixel data, sampling a specific pixel by (x, y), and freeing the resources at shutdown. What's the type of the pixel array and what should I know about it?
evals.expected_output: Uses rl.LoadImage to read the file. Uses rl.LoadImageColors to get a flat array of pixels. Notes the return type is [^]rl.Color — Odin's multi-pointer (typed C-style pointer to many) — and explains that it has no length tag, just the pointer. Notes rl.Color is [4]u8 (or distinct [4]u8) so .r/.g/.b/.a accessors work. Shows row-major indexing: pixels[y * width + x] for sampling. Mentions raylib copies the pixel data, so rl.UnloadImage is safe immediately after LoadImageColors. For shutdown: rl.UnloadImageColors (matches rl.LoadImageColors). May also note bounds-checking caveat for multi-pointers.
expectations: Uses rl.LoadImage to load the PNG into a CPU-side rl.Image
Uses rl.LoadImageColors (or a documented direct cast of img.data) to get an addressable pixel array
Identifies the pixel array type as `[^]rl.Color` — Odin's multi-pointer
Notes that multi-pointers have no length tag and behave like C-style pointers (caller must track bounds)
Notes rl.Color is [4]u8 (or distinct [4]u8) — RGBA 8-bit per channel — and that .r/.g/.b/.a accessors work
Shows row-major indexing: pixels[y * width + x]
Notes raylib copies pixel data (or equivalent), so rl.UnloadImage is safe to call immediately after LoadImageColors
Uses rl.UnloadImageColors at shutdown to free the pixel array
